title: The Case for Continuing Rate Cuts
summary: Waller makes an explicit, strongly dovish case to cut the policy rate 25bp at the December meeting as risk management. He argues that despite the data gaps from the 43-day shutdown, private and partial public data show a labor market that is weak and near stall speed, with job creation he believes is predominantly demand-driven rather than just lower labor supply. He judges underlying inflation (ex-tariffs) close to 2 percent with well-anchored expectations and sees no force that would accelerate inflation. He worries restrictive policy is weighing on lower- and middle-income households and frames a December cut as insurance against further labor-market deterioration and a move toward neutral.

key_quotes.quote: This reading of the data leads me, at this moment, to support a cut in the FOMC's policy rate at our next meeting on December 9 and 10 as a matter of risk management.
key_quotes.context: Direct statement of his dovish near-term call for a December cut.

key_quotes.quote: So, what is that data telling us? First, that the labor market is still weak and near stall speed.
key_quotes.context: Headline read of the labor market driving his policy view.

key_quotes.quote: When outcomes are uncertain, policymakers must manage the risks, and the evidence is pointing toward a greater risk that low job creation is predominantly demand driven.
key_quotes.context: Argues weakness is demand-side, strengthening the case for easing.

key_quotes.quote: With underlying inflation close to the FOMC's target and evidence of a weak labor market, I support cutting the Committee's policy rate by another 25 basis points at our December meeting.
key_quotes.context: Low inflation conviction paired with the cut recommendation.
